svg.text: I LOVE MY LOVE WITH AN A

div.div: Nothing special — just the game board on page 1.

Players sit in a circle. The first player fills in this sentence using words starting with A: 'I love my love with an A because they are [Amusing]; I took them to the sign of the [Anchor] and treated them to [Apricots]; their name is [Agnes] and they come from [Aberdeen].' Player two uses B, player three uses C, and so on through the alphabet. Anyone who hesitates too long, repeats a word already used, or uses the wrong letter is out or must do a quick penalty. Q, X, and Z are the real tests.

I Love My Love is mentioned in Lewis Carroll's Alice Through the Looking-Glass, where the White Queen plays it using the letter 'M' — its inclusion in one of the most beloved Victorian novels made it a must-know parlor game for the era.

Each player in turn must complete the sentence 'I love my love with an [A] because she is [Adorable]; I took her to the sign of [the Anchor] and treated her to [Almonds]; her name is [Alice] and she comes from [Aberdeen]' — using a new letter of the alphabet for each round, supplying all words with the same letter.

svg.text: Source: The Sociable, or One Thousand and One Home Amusements (1858) · Heritage Parlor
